Name: Causal Autoimmune Architecture Platform

schema.name: CaseNarrative
schema.Description: PANEL 1 of the witness (raw leaf, read by nothing downstream): the natural-language intake case the LLM was handed. Every raw observation below is extracted from THIS text; each leaf's SourceQuote points back into it so the extraction is human-checkable independently of the verdict. Synthetic but transparent — invented case, literature-aligned loci/thresholds.

data.Name: Ana Reyes
data.CaseNarrative: Ana Reyes, a 34-year-old woman of European ancestry (cohort is in the training set), presents with photosensitive malar rash, arthralgia, and a positive ANA, raising concern for SLE onset. Family history is notable for lupus in a maternal aunt. Genotyping finds the IRF5 regulatory variant rs2004640 at an allele frequency of 0.006 (rare), and allele-specific expression is present at the locus. Functional workup of the IRF5 -> type-I interferon -> SLE-risk edge returns a cis-eQTL in blood RNA-seq with effect 0.91 (SE 0.20), a caQTL in blood ATAC-seq with effect 0.73 (SE 0.19), and a pQTL in blood proteomics with effect 0.66 (SE 0.20) — three assays across two omics modalities, all ancestry-PC and batch adjusted. The edge replicates with a positive effect sign in an East Asian cohort (p=0.004) and in an African cohort (p=0.013), while a European replication is directionally consistent but not significant (p=0.18). Two ancestry-permutation negative controls collapse to the null (permutation effect 0.012 and 0.028, well inside the +/-0.1 null band). The IL-interferon target is druggable (anifrolumab, anti-IFNAR1), giving a perturbable intervention target. For SLE-onset risk, the model's reliability bins each carry coverage of 29-34 held-out individuals, with observed event rates tracking predicted bands closely (e.g. 0.09 observed at the 0.1 band, 0.88 at the 0.9 band). No cryptic-relatedness or assortative-mating leakage was flagged.

data.Name: Bili Okafor
data.CaseNarrative: Bili Okafor, a 41-year-old man of European ancestry (in the training set), presents with serositis, fatigue, and a high-titre ANA suggestive of SLE onset. Genotyping finds the IRF5 regulatory variant rs2004640 at an allele frequency of 0.006 (rare), with allele-specific expression present. The IRF5 -> type-I interferon -> SLE-risk edge is supported by a cis-eQTL in blood RNA-seq with effect 0.84 (SE 0.21), a caQTL in blood ATAC-seq with effect 0.79 (SE 0.20), and a pQTL in blood proteomics with effect 0.58 (SE 0.20), spanning two omics modalities and adjusted for ancestry PCs and batch. It replicates positive in an East Asian cohort (p=0.006) and an African cohort (p=0.017), with a non-significant European replication (p=0.22). Two ancestry-permutation controls collapse to the null (permutation effect 0.015 and 0.024 inside +/-0.1). The interferon target is druggable. The mechanism is as solid as Ana's — but the calibration substrate is thin: this site only accrued coverage of 7-14 held-out individuals per reliability bin, far below the 20-count floor needed to trust the predicted-vs-observed match. No cryptic-relatedness leakage was flagged.

data.Name: Chen Wei
data.CaseNarrative: Chen Wei, a 47-year-old woman of East Asian ancestry (in the training set), presents with arthritis, oral ulcers, and anti-dsDNA positivity concerning for SLE onset. Genotyping finds the STAT4 regulatory variant rs7574865 at an allele frequency of 0.008 (rare), with allele-specific expression present. The STAT4 -> Th1/IFN -> SLE-risk edge shows a cis-eQTL in blood RNA-seq with effect 1.02 (SE 0.23), a caQTL in blood ATAC-seq with effect 0.61 (SE 0.20), and a pQTL in blood proteomics with effect 0.55 (SE 0.21), across two modalities, ancestry-PC and batch adjusted. Replication is troubling: the effect is positive in an East Asian cohort (p=0.009) but the SIGN FLIPS to negative in an African cohort (p=0.024), and the European replication is non-significant (p=0.27) — only one concordant cohort. Worse, while one ancestry-permutation control collapses (permutation effect 0.013), a second negative control does NOT collapse: its permutation effect is 0.46, far outside the +/-0.1 null band, so the signal is not separable from permuted noise. Reliability bins carry coverage of 28-32 per bin. No cryptic-relatedness leakage was flagged.

data.Name: Diego Santos
data.CaseNarrative: Diego Santos, a 29-year-old man of European ancestry (in the training set), presents with nephritis and a malar rash concerning for SLE onset. Genotyping finds the IRF5 regulatory variant rs2004640 at an allele frequency of 0.006 (rare), with allele-specific expression present. The IRF5 -> type-I interferon -> SLE-risk edge is fully supported: a cis-eQTL in blood RNA-seq with effect 0.88 (SE 0.20), a caQTL in blood ATAC-seq with effect 0.95 (SE 0.21), and a pQTL in blood proteomics with effect 0.62 (SE 0.20), across two modalities and adjusted for ancestry PCs and batch. It replicates positive in an East Asian cohort (p=0.003) and an African cohort (p=0.019), with a non-significant European replication (p=0.16). Two ancestry-permutation controls collapse to the null (permutation effect 0.009 and 0.031 inside +/-0.1). The interferon target is druggable, and reliability bins carry coverage of 30-36 per bin. However, sample QC raised a cryptic-relatedness / assortative-mating flag for this participant: kinship estimates indicate undeclared relatives inside the cohort, so the prediction may be confounded by leakage rather than the mechanism alone.

data.Name: Esi Mensah
data.CaseNarrative: Esi Mensah, a 38-year-old woman of African ancestry (in the training set), presents with arthralgia and cytopenias concerning for SLE onset. Genotyping finds the CTLA4 enhancer variant rs3087243 at an allele frequency of 0.007 (rare), with allele-specific expression present. The CTLA4 -> T-cell costimulation -> SLE-risk edge is well supported: a cis-eQTL in blood RNA-seq with effect 0.79 (SE 0.21), a caQTL in blood ATAC-seq with effect 0.71 (SE 0.20), and a pQTL in blood proteomics with effect 0.41 (SE 0.20), across two modalities, ancestry-PC and batch adjusted. It replicates positive in an East Asian cohort (p=0.007) and a European cohort (p=0.021), with a non-significant African replication (p=0.19). Two ancestry-permutation controls collapse to the null (permutation effect 0.011 and 0.026 inside +/-0.1), and reliability bins carry coverage of 27-33 per bin. The gap is experimental: no perturbable intervention target has been mapped for this edge — there is no agent or assay that could knock the costimulation node down to test it, so the mechanism is not experimentally falsifiable as stated.

data.Name: Faisal Haidar
data.CaseNarrative: Faisal Haidar, a 31-year-old man of Indigenous American ancestry — an ancestry deliberately HELD OUT of the training set — presents with dactylitis, enthesitis, and nail pitting concerning for psoriatic-arthritis (PsA) onset. Genotyping finds the IL23R regulatory variant rs11209026 at an allele frequency of 0.009 (rare), with allele-specific expression present. The IL23R -> IL-17 axis -> PsA-risk edge is well supported: a cis-eQTL in blood RNA-seq with effect 1.07 (SE 0.20), a caQTL in blood ATAC-seq with effect 0.83 (SE 0.21), and a pQTL in blood proteomics with effect 0.74 (SE 0.20), across two modalities and adjusted for ancestry PCs and batch. The IL-17 target is druggable (secukinumab). Two ancestry-permutation controls collapse to the null (permutation effect 0.008 and 0.022 inside +/-0.1), and reliability bins carry coverage of 30-35 per bin. But every replication of this edge was run in an Indigenous American cohort: p=0.005, p=0.015, and p=0.034, all positive, all in the SAME ancestry. There is no cohort in a DIFFERENT ancestry confirming the effect — so for a patient whose ancestry is absent from training, nothing demonstrates the mechanism transports. No cryptic-relatedness leakage was flagged.

data.Name: Grace Lin
data.CaseNarrative: Grace Lin, a 36-year-old woman of Indigenous American ancestry — again an ancestry HELD OUT of the training set — presents with plaque psoriasis and asymmetric oligoarthritis concerning for psoriatic-arthritis (PsA) onset. Genotyping finds the IL23R regulatory variant rs11209026 at an allele frequency of 0.009 (rare), with allele-specific expression present. The IL23R -> IL-17 axis -> PsA-risk edge is well supported: a cis-eQTL in blood RNA-seq with effect 0.96 (SE 0.20), a caQTL in blood ATAC-seq with effect 0.88 (SE 0.21), and a pQTL in blood proteomics with effect 0.69 (SE 0.20), across two modalities and adjusted for ancestry PCs and batch. The IL-17 target is druggable (secukinumab). Two ancestry-permutation controls collapse to the null (permutation effect 0.007 and 0.019 inside +/-0.1), and reliability bins carry coverage of 30-36 per bin. Crucially — and unlike Faisal — this edge was MEASURED to replicate ACROSS ancestries: positive in a European cohort (p=0.004), positive in an East Asian cohort (p=0.012), and positive in an Indigenous American cohort (p=0.029). Because the mechanism demonstrably holds in ancestries other than the held-out one, the prediction transports. No cryptic-relatedness leakage was flagged.

schema.Description: Derived bounded blend of qualified-evidence count, modality breadth, replication rate, and control survival.
schema.formula: =IF(((0.30 * IF({{CountQualifiedEvidence}} >= 4, 1, {{CountQualifiedEvidence}} / 4)) + (0.20 * IF({{CountModalitiesSupporting}} >= 3, 1, {{CountModalitiesSupporting}} / 3)) + (0.30 * {{ReplicationFraction}}) + (0.20 * IF({{SurvivesNegativeControls}}, 1, 0))) > 1, 1, (0.30 * IF({{CountQualifiedEvidence}} >= 4, 1, {{CountQualifiedEvidence}} / 4)) + (0.20 * IF({{CountModalitiesSupporting}} >= 3, 1, {{CountModalitiesSupporting}} / 3)) + (0.30 * {{ReplicationFraction}}) + (0.20 * IF({{SurvivesNegativeControls}}, 1, 0)))

schema.Description: Derived: a confirmed node whose effect replicated in >=1 different ancestry (measured invariance).

Axioms.Description: Non-negotiable invariants the platform must obey. Load-bearing constraints, not per-loop work. Captured from the gauntlet conversation.

schema.name: AxiomId
schema.Description: Stable identifier for the axiom.

schema.name: Statement
schema.Description: The invariant, stated as a rule.

schema.name: Rationale
schema.Description: Why this must hold / what it defends against.

schema.name: Category
schema.Description: Grouping: trust-boundary | solve-by-inference | knob | witness | scope | report-derivation.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Path to this Axioms entry: /admin/axioms/<id>.

data.Statement: The LLM never gets a vote on the conclusion.
data.Rationale: Demotes the model to intake-clerk + lab; the verdict is the deterministic DAG's, never the model's.
data.Name: The LLM never gets a vote on the conclusion.

data.Statement: IsClinicallyActionable is computed, never entered.
data.Rationale: No field a curator (human or LLM) can set to make the diagnosis come out right.
data.Name: IsClinicallyActionable is computed, never entered.

data.Statement: The trust boundary is a line in the DAG: fields with a formula are the model's; raw-input leaf fields are the LLM's (or a human's).
data.Rationale: Makes the boundary mechanically checkable rather than a vibe.
data.Name: The trust boundary is a line in the DAG: fields with a formula are the model's; raw-input leaf fields are the LLM's (or a human's).

data.Statement: The LLM does exactly two things, both at the leaf layer: interpret intake facts, and produce synthetic-but-transparent test results.
data.Rationale: Intake clerk + lab — the un-alarming medical roles. It computes no higher-order inference.
data.Name: The LLM does exactly two things, both at the leaf layer: interpret intake facts, and produce synthetic-but-transparent test results.

data.Statement: Every choice the LLM makes is a literal, editable knob in the admin UI.
data.Rationale: The model's judgment is fully externalized; a wrong input is nudged, not re-prompted.
data.Name: Every choice the LLM makes is a literal, editable knob in the admin UI.

data.Statement: Every DAG formula is inspectable and correctable in place, like a spreadsheet cell.
data.Rationale: Editing a value or formula body is local to its dependency subtree; only retyping/regraphing ripples.
data.Name: Every DAG formula is inspectable and correctable in place, like a spreadsheet cell.

data.Statement: Extraction and derivation are verifiable independently (three-panel witness).
data.Rationale: Input case text -> extracted facts w/ provenance -> deterministic diagnosis. Defeats the laundering objection.
data.Name: Extraction and derivation are verifiable independently (three-panel witness).

data.Statement: The same deterministic machine must land on either side and name the single deciding gate.
data.Rationale: An engine that only ever says "actionable" proves nothing.
data.Name: The same deterministic machine must land on either side and name the single deciding gate.

data.Statement: The diagnosis report is derived, never authored as prose; it is structured natural language assembled from the DAG.
data.Rationale: A prose writeup would be a second, unwitnessed surface where a hallucination could hide. The report must be a deterministic projection of facts already in the graph, the same way Postgres views are.
data.Name: The diagnosis report is derived, never authored as prose; it is structured natural language assembled from the DAG.

data.Statement: Every word the patient gave us, and every node linked to that patient in the DAG down to ground truth, is represented somewhere in that patient's report.
data.Rationale: Completeness is the whole point: nothing the patient said and no derived fact may silently vanish from the writeup. If a value mattered enough to enter the DAG, it earns a sentence (or a deliberate, predicate-suppressed absence).
data.Name: Every word the patient gave us, and every node linked to that patient in the DAG down to ground truth, is represented somewhere in that patient's report.

data.Statement: No single piece of report language is more complex than one spreadsheet cell: it references sibling values directly and does no joins or group-bys.
data.Rationale: CMCC/SDLAF discipline. A narrative fragment is a calculated field on a row; it reads other fields of that same row (and global phrases from __meta), never traverses or aggregates. Complex sentences are decomposed into constituent fragment fields, each one cell-simple.
data.Name: No single piece of report language is more complex than one spreadsheet cell: it references sibling values directly and does no joins or group-bys.

data.Statement: A fragment's language only appears when its predicates hold for that row; irrelevant language is suppressed per case.
data.Rationale: Case-by-case, not a globally imposed template. The same field yields "normal temperature", "light fever", or "severe fever" depending on the row's own value; a fragment whose predicate is false renders empty and contributes nothing to that patient's report.
data.Name: A fragment's language only appears when its predicates hold for that row; irrelevant language is suppressed per case.

data.Statement: Complex language is built from intermediate translation fields: calculated text fragments composed from other predicates on the same row, then composed upward.
data.Rationale: The way to keep every piece cell-simple is to pull a complex sentence apart into its constituent fragments, each its own field, and let higher fragments reference lower ones. The report is the top of that composition, not a monolith.
data.Name: Complex language is built from intermediate translation fields: calculated text fragments composed from other predicates on the same row, then composed upward.

data.Statement: Common, reusable language lives once in the __meta settings table and is referenced by fragments; it is not re-implemented per case.
data.Rationale: Literal global phrases ("The patient presented with…") and shared banding vocabulary belong in __meta so they are authored and corrected in one place. Fragments reference them; the per-row logic stays contextual while the wording stays DRY.
data.Name: Common, reusable language lives once in the __meta settings table and is referenced by fragments; it is not re-implemented per case.

data.Statement: The schema may be expressed in RuleSpeak-style language, but the diagnosis report itself is derived (rendered), not declared.
data.Rationale: RuleSpeak documents the rules; it is not the patient-facing artifact. The report is a rendered projection of each patient's row values through the fragment fields — generated by a thin renderer that only concatenates already-derived, predicate-gated fragments in order.
data.Name: The schema may be expressed in RuleSpeak-style language, but the diagnosis report itself is derived (rendered), not declared.

data.Claim: Five of seven patients fail, each on exactly one gate.
data.HowWitnessed: Coverage matrix in scenarios.md; L6/L7 harness assert the deciding gate per patient.
data.Name: Five of seven patients fail, each on exactly one gate.

data.Claim: The same machine produces TRUE for {A,G} and FALSE for {B,C,D,E,F}.
data.HowWitnessed: L7-cohort.test.js asserts exactly {A,G} actionable as a set.
data.Name: The same machine produces TRUE for {A,G} and FALSE for {B,C,D,E,F}.

data.Claim: Raw facts alone derive the keystone with no hand-entered answer anywhere.
data.HowWitnessed: Every leaf in the harness is a raw observation; keystone == AND(gates) cross-check.
data.Name: Raw facts alone derive the keystone with no hand-entered answer anywhere.

data.Claim: Extracted leaf facts are checkable against the source case text, independently of the verdict.
data.HowWitnessed: Three-panel witness view: per-fact provenance pointer back into the case text.
data.Name: Extracted leaf facts are checkable against the source case text, independently of the verdict.

data.Claim: All seven oracle patients resolve to their expected keystone value.
data.HowWitnessed: Build verification asserts the scenarios.md oracle against vw_individualpredictions.
data.Name: All seven oracle patients resolve to their expected keystone value.

data.Claim: Each derived node carries an expert-verifiable line of reasoning.
data.HowWitnessed: Derivation trace from derivation-spec.md printed in every test name/failure.
data.Name: Each derived node carries an expert-verifiable line of reasoning.

data.Claim: Every word the patient gave us appears somewhere in that patient's derived report (no patient input is dropped).
data.HowWitnessed: Coverage check: tokenize each patient's source intake text; assert each meaningful token is traceable into at least one rendered report fragment (directly quoted or its derived translation).
data.Name: Every word the patient gave us appears somewhere in that patient's derived report (no patient input is dropped).

data.Claim: Every DAG node linked to a patient, down to ground truth, is represented somewhere in that patient's report.
data.HowWitnessed: Walk the patient's subgraph (keystone -> gates -> evidence -> raw leaves); assert each visited node contributes at least one rendered fragment, or is deliberately suppressed by a false predicate that the report names.
data.Name: Every DAG node linked to a patient, down to ground truth, is represented somewhere in that patient's report.

data.Claim: No narrative fragment field traverses, joins, or aggregates; each reads only sibling fields of its own row plus __meta__ phrases.
data.HowWitnessed: Static lint over fragment formulas: reject any formula that references another table or a rollup/lookup across rows; fragments may reference {{SiblingField}} and __meta__ keys only.
data.Name: No narrative fragment field traverses, joins, or aggregates; each reads only sibling fields of its own row plus __meta__ phrases.

data.Claim: The same patient row always renders the byte-identical report; the report is a pure projection of the DAG with no model in the loop.
data.HowWitnessed: Render each patient twice and diff; assert zero difference and that the renderer makes no LLM/network calls.
data.Name: The same patient row always renders the byte-identical report; the report is a pure projection of the DAG with no model in the loop.

data.Title: LLM intake clerk
data.Description: LLM reads a natural-language case and writes raw intake observation rows (ancestry, allele freqs, presenting facts) to base tables.
data.Name: LLM intake clerk

data.Title: Synthetic-but-transparent lab generator
data.Description: LLM emits the case's test results (effect sizes/SEs, replication signs/p-values, permutation effect sizes, calibration coverage) as visible, sourced, editable leaf rows.
data.Name: Synthetic-but-transparent lab generator

data.Title: Three-panel witness view
data.Description: Input case text | extracted facts with provenance | deterministic diagnosis + four-gate trace.
data.Name: Three-panel witness view

data.Title: Knob-editing admin UI
data.Description: Every LLM-produced leaf value is an editable field; correcting it re-derives the DAG.
data.Name: Knob-editing admin UI

data.Title: Gate explainability
data.Description: For a patient, show why each gate passed/failed by walking one level down (coverage, cross-ancestry count, etc.).
data.Name: Gate explainability

data.Title: Per-patient diagnosis writeup
data.Description: Doctor-style Markdown writeup (-> diagnosis.pdf once green) produced as an artifact of a passing run.
data.Name: Per-patient diagnosis writeup

data.Title: Derived narrative fragment fields
data.Description: Per-row calculated text fields that turn each fact into structured natural language, predicate-gated and case-by-case (e.g. Individual.TemperatureNarrative -> 'normal temperature' | 'light fever' | 'severe fever' from the row's own value). Each fragment is one spreadsheet-cell formula: it reads sibling fields and __meta__ phrases only, no joins or aggregations. Complex sentences are decomposed into constituent intermediate-translation fragments composed upward.
data.Name: Derived narrative fragment fields

data.Title: Global phrasebook in __meta__
data.Description: Reusable, contextual language atoms stored once in the __meta__ settings table — literal global phrases ('The patient presented with…') and shared banding vocabulary — referenced by fragment fields so common language is authored/corrected in one place, never re-implemented per case.
data.Name: Global phrasebook in __meta__

data.Title: Thin Python report renderer (replaces handlebars)
data.Description: Replaces the json-hbars-transform plan step with a small Python renderer whose only job is to read each patient's derived fragment fields from vw_* views and concatenate the non-empty, predicate-gated fragments in order into the diagnosis report (Markdown -> diagnosis.pdf). All sentence wording lives in the rulebook as calculated fields; the renderer owns no business or narrative logic.
data.Name: Thin Python report renderer (replaces handlebars)

data.Title: Report total-coverage harness
data.Description: Tests that every patient word and every linked DAG node down to ground truth is represented in that patient's report (see tfs-report-covers-every-word, tfs-report-covers-every-dag-node).
data.Name: Report total-coverage harness

data.Definition: A literal, editable leaf value in the admin UI representing one choice the LLM (or a human) made; nudged, not re-prompted, when wrong.
data.Name: knob

data.Definition: The only two roles the LLM plays: interpret intake facts (clerk) and produce the case's test results (lab). Never the diagnostician.
data.Name: intake clerk + lab

data.Definition: The trust boundary, stated mechanically: fields with a formula belong to the model; raw-input leaf fields are the LLM's or a human's.
data.Name: line in the DAG

data.Definition: Input case text | extracted facts with per-fact provenance | deterministic diagnosis + gate trace — the two halves checkable independently.
data.Name: three-panel witness

data.Definition: Test results are synthetic (the case is invented) but every number is visible, sourced to the case, and editable; nothing is hidden.
data.Name: fake-but-transparent

data.Definition: The skeptic's claim that a deterministic function over LLM-chosen inputs just launders a hallucination; defeated by externalized knobs + independent witness.
data.Name: laundering objection

data.Definition: The v2 layer the audit asked for: disease modeled as a WITNESSED state machine whose transitions fire only on raw longitudinal observations (rising anti-dsDNA, falling C3/C4, proteinuria). Current state and dwell-time are derived & bitemporal — the contrast the audit drew with an 'evidence gate'.
data.Name: disease state simulator

data.Definition: The v1 layer: per-patient adjudication of whether a prediction is trustworthy enough to act on (the four gates + IsClinicallyActionable). v2 keeps this and adds the disease-state simulator above it on the same hub.
data.Name: evidence gate

data.Definition: A state machine on the polymorphic StateMachines substrate whose subject is a patient's disease course (presymptomatic -> serologic-active -> early-nephritis -> renal-flare-risk -> biopsy-indicated). Transitions are raw-leaf-triggered; current state is the derived IsCurrent occupancy.
data.Name: disease progression machine

data.Definition: Bitemporal derivation: how long a subject has been in a disease state (now - EnteredAt while current; ExitedAt - EnteredAt once left). The audit asked for 'how long has the patient/disease been in a state' — this is that, as a formula.
data.Name: dwell time

data.Definition: The raw longitudinal leaves driving progression: anti-dsDNA titre trend (rising/stable/falling), complement C3/C4 trend, proteinuria. The LLM/lab may emit these; everything above them is derived.
data.Name: serology trajectory

data.Definition: Derived field: given the patient's confirmed causal mechanism + current disease state, the recommended therapy (MMF vs belimumab vs anifrolumab) and the single deciding reason. The audit's second worked example.
data.Name: treatment line recommendation

data.Definition: The cohort-level surface where causal-hypothesis patterns EMERGE across many patients (vs v1's per-patient verdicts). Discovery is inherently corpus-level; this is the v2 reframe of the audit's 'you don't do discovery' critique.
data.Name: corpus discovery

__meta__.Description: General-purpose, global name/value settings ENTITY — deliberately transpiler-IGNORED (leading underscore). The one place for random/global/third-party/configuration values, choices, parameters, thresholds, and the reusable phrase atoms the derived report draws on. Just global key/value configuration data; that's it. Distinct from the `_meta` conversion-metadata section, and NOT a domain table (no FKs in, nothing aggregates over it, not in the DAG). Each row is one key/value setting. Narrative fragment fields on domain rows reference these keys so common language and shared numbers are authored and corrected once, never re-implemented per case. NOTE: banding-threshold rows (e.g. the temperature bands) are the numeric atoms; the per-case decision of which band applies lives in a calculated fragment field on the patient row, not here — this table holds the reusable pieces, not the case logic. See the effortless-meta-table skill.

schema.name: MetaKey
schema.Description: Stable, unique key for this setting (kebab or dotted). Fragment formulas reference it by this key.

schema.name: Category
schema.Description: Grouping: report-config | phrase | band-threshold | band-vocabulary | third-party | parameter | choice.

schema.name: Value
schema.Description: The setting's value as text (numbers and booleans stored as strings; ValueType records the intended type).

schema.name: ValueType
schema.Description: How to read Value: string | number | boolean | json | phrase.

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: What this setting is for and where it is referenced.

schema.name: Source
schema.Description: Provenance: where the value came from (case author, clinical convention, third-party doc, build-seed).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Path to this __meta__ entry: /admin/meta/<key>.

data.Description: The diagnosis report is rendered by a thin Python renderer that concatenates already-derived, predicate-gated narrative fragment fields. Replaces the json-hbars-transform plan step. The renderer holds no narrative or business logic.
Report output format. markdown now -> pdf (diagnosis.pdf) once all harness tests are green.
Separator the renderer places between non-empty fragments when assembling a section (blank line = new paragraph).
When a fragment's predicate is false it renders empty; the renderer drops empty fragments so irrelevant language never appears for that case.
Literal global opener reused across every case's presenting-facts fragment. Authored once here, never re-typed per case.
Connector that introduces the derived conclusion sentence in the keystone fragment.
Reused phrase that names the one gate that decided the keystone, per ax-both-verdicts.
Reused fallback sentence for a section whose predicates all suppressed (used only where a deliberate, named absence is wanted rather than silence).

Lower bound (degF, inclusive) of the 'normal temperature' band. Referenced by the patient's TemperatureNarrative fragment to decide which band's language applies for that case. Illustrative of the banding pattern; replace with the clinically agreed value.
Upper bound (degF, inclusive) of the 'normal temperature' band; above this and up to fever-high is a light fever.
Upper bound (degF) of the 'light fever' band; strictly above this is a 'severe fever'.

Language emitted when the patient's temperature falls in the normal band. The TemperatureNarrative fragment picks one of the three band phrases by comparing the row's own value against the band-threshold rows.
Language emitted for the light-fever band (normal-high < temp <= fever-high).
Language emitted for the severe-fever band (temp > fever-high).
Total-coverage contract toggle: assert every meaningful patient token is traceable into a rendered fragment (tfs-report-covers-every-word).
Total-coverage contract toggle: assert every DAG node linked to the patient down to ground truth contributes a fragment or a named suppression (tfs-report-covers-every-dag-node).

_meta._CMCC_Summary: Loop 0: solve-by-inference rulebook for the Causal Autoimmune Architecture Platform. The keystone IndividualPredictions.IsClinicallyActionable is derived through a calculated-field DAG bottoming out only in raw observations. The per-patient diagnosis report is a derived, total-coverage projection: every patient word and every linked DAG node down to ground truth renders as predicate-gated structured natural language assembled from per-row narrative fragment fields (each one spreadsheet-cell simple), drawing reusable phrases and band thresholds from the __meta__ settings table.

data.Definition: Renal involvement of SLE; immune-complex glomerulonephritis. v2 models its PROGRESSION as a witnessed state machine driven by rising anti-dsDNA + falling complement + proteinuria.
data.Name: Lupus Nephritis

data.Definition: Which therapy to choose given the patient's causal mechanism + disease state: mycophenolate (MMF) vs belimumab vs anifrolumab. v2 derives a recommended line + single deciding reason.
data.Name: Treatment-Line Selection

data.Definition: CNS/PNS manifestations of SLE (seizure, psychosis, cognitive). Modeled as a phenotype subtype carrying a SeverityScore and organ-domain tag.
data.Name: Neuropsychiatric Lupus (NPSLE)

data.Definition: Skin-limited or skin-predominant lupus (acute/subacute/chronic discoid). Phenotype subtype with organ-domain = skin.
data.Name: Cutaneous Lupus

data.Definition: Irreversible accumulated damage, SLICC-style. Modeled as a monotonic damage-accrual score distinct from reversible disease activity.
data.ChallengeStressorType: disease progression / feedback between progression and molecular state
data.Name: Organ Damage (SLICC/ACR Damage Index)

data.Definition: RF/anti-CCP-positive RA; stronger HLA shared-epitope + PTPN22 association, more erosive. Phenotype subtype with a serostatus tag.
data.Name: Seropositive RA (ACPA/RF+)

data.Definition: Autoantibody-negative RA; distinct genetic architecture and trajectory. Phenotype subtype.
data.Name: Seronegative RA (ACPA/RF-)

data.Definition: Radiographic bone erosion; a structural-damage progression axis for RA, distinct from disease activity.
data.Name: Erosive Disease

data.Definition: Spinal/sacroiliac involvement of PsA. Phenotype subtype with axial domain tag.
data.Name: Axial Psoriatic Arthritis

data.Definition: Inflammation at tendon/ligament insertions; hallmark of PsA/SpA. Phenotype subtype.
data.Name: Enthesitis

data.Definition: 'Sausage digit' — whole-digit swelling. PsA phenotype subtype.
data.Name: Dactylitis

data.Definition: Inflammatory eye disease overlapping spondyloarthritis. Phenotype subtype / extra-articular domain.
data.Name: Uveitis

data.Definition: Gut inflammation overlapping spondyloarthritis (IL-23/IL-17 axis shared). Phenotype subtype representing cross-disease pleiotropy.
data.Name: Inflammatory Bowel Disease Overlap

data.Definition: Episodic relapse of disease activity. v2 models flare-risk as a derived state on the progression machine, driven by serology trajectory.
data.ChallengeStressorType: feedback between disease progression and molecular state
data.Name: Flare Pattern

data.Definition: Composite activity indices (SLEDAI-2K for SLE, DAS28 for RA). v2 DERIVES an activity tier from raw component observations rather than hand-entering it.
data.Name: Disease-Activity Score (SLEDAI / DAS28)

data.Definition: First-line / second-line / advanced-therapy sequencing. Modeled as an ordinal on therapies feeding treatment-line selection.
data.Name: Treatment Line

data.Definition: Monitoring for therapy harms. Present as a concept/stressor type; the keystone already carries HasAdverseEffect at the leaf. Deep AE modeling is out-of-scope for the POC.
data.Name: Adverse-Event Surveillance

data.Definition: Entry into specialist care. Represented as the initial state of the clinical-workflow machine.
data.Name: Workflow: Referral

data.Definition: Weighing competing diagnoses. Workflow state.

data.Definition: Ordering the labs/imaging that BECOME the raw leaves. Workflow state.

data.Definition: Applying classification criteria (e.g. EULAR/ACR). Workflow state.

data.Definition: Establishing baseline activity/damage. Workflow state; consumes the derived activity score.
data.Name: Workflow: Baseline Severity Assessment

data.Definition: Selecting therapy; consumes the treatment-line-selection DAG. Workflow state.
data.Name: Workflow: Treatment Choice

data.Definition: Longitudinal follow-up; produces the serology trajectory leaves. Workflow state.
data.Name: Workflow: Monitoring

data.Definition: Responding to a flare. Workflow state tied to the flare-risk progression state.
